name: Republic of Tatarstan (Республика Татарстан / Татарстан Җөмһүрияте)
summary: The Republic of Tatarstan (Russian: Республика Татарстан Respublika Tatarstan / Tatar: Татарстан Җөмһүрияте Tatarstan Cömhüriyäte) is a Volga Russian civil-law constituent republic of the Russian Federation — structurally distinctive globally as the second-largest Russian sub-national republic by population (~3.9 million — second-largest of all 21 Russian republics after Bashkortostan ~4.0 million), as the only Russian sub-national republic to refuse to sign the original Russian Federation Treaty 1992 (Russian Federation Treaty signed 31 March 1992 — Tatarstan + Chechnya were only two Russian republics refusing initial signature, with Tatarstan subsequently signing bilateral Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 15 February 1994 as substitute framework — distinct from rest-of-Russian-republics framework), as the only Russian sub-national republic with formal post-Soviet bilateral power-sharing treaty framework (Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 1994 effective 15 February 1994 + Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 2007 effective 26 June 2007 - 2017 expired — only modern Russian sub-national republic with formal bilateral power-sharing treaty framework, expired 24 July 2017 with no replacement framework as of 2026), and as the central jurisdiction of historical Kazan Khanate 1438-1552 framework (Kazan Khanate established 1438 by Ulugh Muhammad as Golden Horde successor state + Kazan Khanate conquest by Ivan IV the Terrible 2 October 1552 — only modern Russian sub-national republic with continuous Golden-Horde-successor-state historical-cultural-political-Islamic framework). Tatarstan historically operated under Volga Bulgaria 7th-13th centuries + Golden Horde 1242-1438 + Kazan Khanate 1438-1552 + Russian Empire 1552-1917 + Soviet Tatar ASSR 1920-1990 + Republic of Tatarstan 1990+ (with formal sovereignty declaration 30 August 1990). Tatarstan comprises ~67,847 km² with a population of ~3.9 million (~53% Tatar + ~39% Russian + others). Family-law framework operates under the federal Russian civil-law framework (Russian Family Code 1995 + Russian Civil Code 1994) applied uniformly across Russian sub-national territories. The Supreme Court of Tatarstan is the apex regional court; final appellate jurisdiction lies with the Supreme Court of the Russian Federation. Tatarstan is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label. Russia is a Hague Convention 1980 signatory and party (acceded 28 July 2011 effective 1 October 2011) — Tatarstan is governed by Russian Hague-Convention-1980 framework.

statutory_framework.citation: Constitution of the Republic of Tatarstan 1992 (amended 2002)
statutory_framework.title: Constitution of Tatarstan 1992
statutory_framework.relevance: Tatarstan Constitution adopted 6 November 1992 + amended 2002 — formal constitutional framework for Republic of Tatarstan, originally with formal sovereignty declaration framework, amended 2002 to align with Russian Constitution.

statutory_framework.title: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 1994
statutory_framework.relevance: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty signed 15 February 1994 — formal bilateral power-sharing treaty framework establishing Tatarstan-Russian Federation relationship, substitute for unsigned 1992 Russian Federation Treaty.

statutory_framework.title: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 2007
statutory_framework.relevance: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty signed 26 June 2007 — renewed bilateral power-sharing treaty framework, expired 24 July 2017 with no replacement framework as of 2026.

statutory_framework.citation: Constitution of the Russian Federation 1993 Article 5 (Republics)
statutory_framework.title: Constitution of Russian Federation Article 5
statutory_framework.relevance: Russian Constitutional Article 5 establishing republics as constituent subjects of Russian Federation — applicable to Tatarstan.

statutory_framework.citation: Russian Family Code 1995 + Russian Civil Code 1994
statutory_framework.title: Russian Family Code + Civil Code
statutory_framework.relevance: Russian Federal Family Code 1995 + Russian Federal Civil Code 1994 applicable uniformly across Tatarstan.

statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980 — Russia
statutory_framework.relevance: Russian Hague Convention 1980 accession 28 July 2011 effective 1 October 2011 — applicable to Tatarstan.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of the Republic of Tatarstan (Верховный суд Республики Татарстан)
apex_courts.role: Apex regional appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Tatarstan.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of the Russian Federation (Верховный Суд Российской Федерации)
apex_courts.role: Final appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Tatarstan.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Health of the Republic of Tatarstan
professional_regulators.role: Tatarstan regional-level + Russian Federal professional regulatory framework applicable in Tatarstan.

anonymisation_convention: Tatarstan family-court decisions are anonymised per Russian court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Kazan Khanate established
key_developments.description: Kazan Khanate established 1438 by Ulugh Muhammad as Golden Horde successor state — beginning of formal Kazan Khanate framework 1438-1552.

key_developments.title: Kazan Khanate conquest by Ivan IV
key_developments.description: Kazan Khanate conquest by Ivan IV the Terrible 2 October 1552 — formal incorporation into Russian Empire framework 1552-1917.

key_developments.title: Tatar ASSR established
key_developments.description: Soviet Tatar Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic established 27 May 1920 — first formal Tatar autonomous sub-national framework.

key_developments.title: Tatarstan sovereignty declaration
key_developments.description: Tatarstan sovereignty declaration 30 August 1990 — formal post-Soviet sovereignty framework.

key_developments.title: Russian Federation Treaty refusal + Tatarstan Constitution
key_developments.description: Russian Federation Treaty signed 31 March 1992 — Tatarstan + Chechnya were only two Russian republics refusing initial signature + Tatarstan Constitution adopted 6 November 1992.

key_developments.title: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty
key_developments.description: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty signed 15 February 1994 — formal bilateral power-sharing treaty framework establishing Tatarstan-Russian Federation relationship.

key_developments.title: Tatarstan Constitution amendment
key_developments.description: Tatarstan Constitution amended 2002 to align with Russian Constitution — formal post-Putin-recentralisation framework.

key_developments.title: Renewed Power-Sharing Treaty
key_developments.description: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty signed 26 June 2007 — renewed bilateral power-sharing treaty framework.

key_developments.title: Power-Sharing Treaty expiration
key_developments.description: Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 2007 expired 24 July 2017 with no replacement framework — end of formal Russian-Tatarstan bilateral power-sharing treaty framework.

structural_findings: Tatarstan operates a federal Russian civil-law constituent republic framework — places Tatarstan in the Volga Russian Republic cluster.
Largest Russian sub-national republic by population (~3.9 million) is structurally distinctive within Russian republic cluster — second-largest by population after Bashkortostan.

Only Russian sub-national republic to refuse to sign original Russian Federation Treaty 1992 is structurally distinctive globally — Tatarstan + Chechnya were only two Russian republics refusing initial signature.

Only Russian sub-national republic with formal post-Soviet bilateral power-sharing treaty framework is structurally distinctive globally — Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 1994 + 2007 framework, expired 2017 with no replacement framework.

Central jurisdiction of historical Kazan Khanate 1438-1552 framework is structurally distinctive globally — only modern Russian sub-national republic with continuous Golden-Horde-successor-state historical-cultural-political-Islamic framework.

30 August 1990 Tatarstan sovereignty declaration framework is structurally distinctive globally — only modern Russian sub-national territory with continuous formal sovereignty declaration framework predating Soviet dissolution.

1552 Kazan Khanate conquest by Ivan IV historical framework is structurally distinctive globally — only modern Russian sub-national republic with formal Ivan-the-Terrible-conquest historical framework.

sources.title: Government of the Republic of Tatarstan
State Council of the Republic of Tatarstan

editorial_notes: Republic of Tatarstan jurisdiction sidecar — civil-law Volga Russian constituent republic (Constitution of Tatarstan 1992 + Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 1994 + Russian-Tatarstan Power-Sharing Treaty 2007 + Constitution of Russian Federation 1993 Article 5 + Russian Family Code 1995 + Kazan Khanate 1438-1552 + Volga Bulgaria 7th-13th centuries + Golden Horde 1242-1438 + Russian Empire 1552-1917 + Tatar ASSR 1920-1990 + 1990 sovereignty declaration + 1992 RF Treaty refusal + 2017 Power-Sharing Treaty expiration + Russian Hague-1980). Largest Russian sub-national republic by population + only Russian sub-national republic to refuse to sign original Russian Federation Treaty 1992 + only Russian sub-national republic with formal post-Soviet bilateral power-sharing treaty framework + central jurisdiction of historical Kazan Khanate 1438-1552 framework + 30 August 1990 Tatarstan sovereignty declaration framework + 1552 Kazan Khanate conquest by Ivan IV historical framework.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.

Joins Volga + civil-law + Russian-Republic cluster + largest-Russian-Republic-by-population + Russian-Tatarstan-Power-Sharing-Treaty-1994-2007-2017-globally-distinctive + 1992-Russian-Federation-Treaty-refusal + Kazan-Khanate-1438-1552-Golden-Horde-successor-state + 1552-Ivan-the-Terrible-conquest + 1990-sovereignty-declaration + Tatar-Muslim-cultural-political-identity + Russian-Hague-1980-2011 clusters within the corpus.
